one_line_takeaway: Auditing a base model tells you little about the safety of its benignly fine-tuned domain derivatives: safety drift is large, bidirectional, unpredictable from engineering choices, and confounded by benchmarks that disagree with each other.
key_finding: Benign fine-tuning does NOT preserve safety and its effect is largely unpredictable. In the ecological set 81% of medical models and 93% of legal models (with full instrument set) showed mixed-sign drift, improving on one benchmark while degrading on another. Under fully controlled Phase II conditions the inconsistency persisted: 83% of medical configs improved on in-domain benchmarks yet 100% degraded on general-purpose MLCommons. Neither fine-tuning method, base family, nor parameter-update magnitude (normalized L2, |rho|<0.25, R^2<0.1) reliably predicted the outcome. Benchmarks disagreed severely for the same models (medical median Spearman rho=0.23; MLCommons vs CARES rho=-0.65), and simply adding examples to the HEx-PHI judge template reversed the sign of 25% of medical results, showing evaluation framing can move measured safety as much as fine-tuning itself.
core_contribution: A two-phase empirical study of how LLM safety alignment shifts after BENIGN domain-specific fine-tuning in medicine and law. Phase I is an ecological audit of real deployed open-weight fine-tunes scraped from Hugging Face (n=31: 16 medical, 15 legal), each compared to its base; Phase II is a controlled experiment fine-tuning 4 instruction-tuned bases on a single high-traffic domain dataset per domain while varying only the fine-tuning method (LoRA/QLoRA/FFT). The paper's own contributions (distinct from the cited prior work on adversarial fine-tuning and Alpaca/Dolly erosion): (1) large-scale evidence that benign, capability-driven fine-tuning induces large, heterogeneous, frequently BIDIRECTIONAL (mixed-sign) safety changes rather than uniform degradation; (2) evidence that common engineering choices (fine-tuning method, base-model family, parameter-update magnitude via normalized L2 distance) have weak predictive power over the direction/magnitude of drift; (3) documentation that measured 'safety' disagrees sharply across benchmarks for the same model, making measurement itself a reliability problem; and (4) governance analysis arguing that EU AI Act compute-fraction thresholds for 'significant modification' are poorly matched to actual safety impact.
threat_model: Not an adversarial-attack paper and it does not posit a malicious actor. The risk studied is UNINTENTIONAL safety drift in the open-weight supply chain: a downstream practitioner adapts a general-purpose model to a high-stakes domain (clinical documentation/triage, contract review, legal drafting) using ordinary benign task data and standard PEFT tooling, and unknowingly ships a derivative whose safety profile diverges from the base that was actually audited. The paper explicitly frames this as a supply-chain reliability / accountability problem spanning upstream providers and downstream deployers, and contrasts it with the adversarial fine-tuning attack vector emphasized in prior work (which they cite but do not study). It also flags a secondary, non-actor threat: governance that trusts base-model evaluations or coarse modification thresholds systematically under-detects these behavioral shifts.
limitations: Authors' own caveats: (1) ecological models differ in undocumented data/training choices, so Phase I cannot establish causal mechanism (motivating Phase II). (2) No external validation against ground-truth harm or expert judgment, so benchmark disagreement cannot be disentangled between genuine construct-validity differences and measurement flaws. (3) Some disagreement is partly artifactual, e.g. SORRY-Bench rewards refusal and thus penalizes the very utility legal fine-tuning aims to add, and SafeLawBench's terse reference-answer template penalizes contextualized legal explanations. Reviewer-noted scope limits: results are on open-weight 2.7B-70B models (controlled arm only 7-9B) with one dataset and 1 epoch per domain; closed-API fine-tuning is only speculated about; heavy reliance on LLM-as-judge (GPT-4o-mini), which the paper itself shows is fragile.

datasets_used: 31 deployed Hugging Face fine-tunes (16 medical, 15 legal; >1.6M downloads, >1,400 citations; ~100 models analyzed in total across both phases)
ai-medical-chatbot (250k patient-doctor dialogues; reused in ~70 downstream fine-tunes)
LawInstruct QA subset (525k examples across 17 jurisdictions)
Benchmarks: HEx-PHI, MLCommons AILuminate demo set, MedSafetyBench, CARES (medical); SafeLawBench, SORRY-Bench legal subset, Trident (legal)
quantitative_results: Medical ecological: ~60% of fine-tunes had lower unsafe rate than base on a given benchmark; 81% showed mixed-sign drift; median change -1.5 to -6.6 pp; CARES dispersion SD~40 pp (range -68.5 to +69 pp)
Base-model unsafety vs post-tuning drift: Pearson r~-0.55, p<0.001 (initially safer models more likely to degrade)
Medical benchmark disagreement: median Spearman rho=0.23; MLCommons vs CARES rho=-0.65

Lineages (n=15): dispersion rose bases->derivatives (Delta-SD HEx-PHI +9.75, MedSafetyBench +11.22, MLCommons +8.75); median absolute drift 7pp (1st tune) -> 13pp (2nd); 60-86% of lineages showed sign reversals between stages

Legal ecological: 60% mixed-sign on unsafe-only benchmarks, 93% with full instrument set; median drift within +/-3 pp; SafeLawBench 73% moved safer; SORRY-Bench/Trident ~47% vs 53% split; inter-benchmark rho~0.06-0.17; fully coherent behavior in only 23% of models

Phase II medical (controlled): 83% of configs improved on in-domain benchmarks, 100% degraded on MLCommons; medians from +12.2 pp improvement (MedSafetyBench) to 26.4 pp degradation (MLCommons); HEx-PHI vs MedSafetyBench rho~0.9, CARES vs MLCommons rho~-0.6
Phase II examples: QLoRA-Gemma CARES -36 pp but MLCommons +45 pp; FFT-Gemma CARES -69.2 pp (largest single improvement); Gemma MLCommons +33 to +45 pp degradation

Parameter-distance proxy failure: normalized L2 vs safety change |rho|<0.25, R^2<0.1 overall; three Mistral variants achieved ~-35 to -37 pp on MedSafetyBench despite L2 spanning ~0.01-0.29; legal HEx-PHI showed rho<-0.64, p=0.02 (smaller updates -> larger degradation)

Phase II legal: MLCommons degraded across all configs (mean Delta=+19.8 pp); SafeLawBench degraded in 92%; 83% mixed-sign overall, dropping to 42% on unsafe-only benchmarks; HEx-PHI vs Trident rho=+0.82, HEx-PHI vs SafeLawBench rho=-0.63, MLCommons vs SORRY-Bench rho=-0.55
Evaluation-modification experiment: adding per-score examples to the HEx-PHI judge template reversed the sign of 25% of medical results while model outputs were held fixed
baselines_compared: Each fine-tuned model vs its own corresponding base model (pairwise safety drift, the primary comparison)
Phase II reference bases: Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct, gemma-2-9b-it, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.1, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
Fine-tuning methods contrasted: LoRA vs QLoRA vs full fine-tuning (FFT)
Instruction-tuned vs unaligned foundation bases (consistent improvement more common when unaligned bases were tuned)
